column6: summary

Agent-specific security, safety, and reliability standard covering data and privacy, security, safety, reliability, accountability, and societal risk. The launch announcement says certification requires 50-plus technical, operational, and legal safeguards plus third-party technical testing.

Certification comprises scoping, evidence collection, technical evaluations, remediation, report sign-off, and certificate issuance. Certificates last 12 months; in-scope agents require quarterly red-teaming to maintain them. AIUC's own pages currently state both four-to-eight and five-to-ten week timelines, an official-source inconsistency this tracker preserves rather than normalizes.

Requires logs of AI processes, actions, and outputs to support incident investigation, audit, and behavior explanation. Expected agent evidence includes provenance metadata, tool-call parameters and results, sub-agent delegations and outcomes, human approval events, and reasoning traces where available, plus retention rules, access controls, PII sanitation, and optional tamper-evidence such as WORM storage and hash chains.
column11: AIUC-1 control E015: log model activity

Insurance: AIUC-1 C007 and C009 human oversight controls
column6: C007 defines high-risk output review workflows with detection thresholds, reviewer assignments, escalation paths, and documented decisions; C009 defines real-time user stop, pause, redirect, feedback, and issue-reporting mechanisms. Both controls are marked optional in the standard.
column11: AIUC-1 control C007: flag high-risk recommendations
column15: Optional controls in the standard, not baseline requirements.

Insurance: AIUC-1 B006 and D003 agent and tool boundaries
column6: B006 requires safeguards preventing agents from acting beyond authorized scope, with service and MCP-server allowlists, gateway restrictions, and boundary-violation logs. D003 requires tool authorization and validation with parameter checks, rate limits, transaction caps, circuit breakers, tool-call logs, and optional human approval for sensitive operations.
column11: AIUC-1 control D003: restrict unsafe tool calls

column6: Requires independent testing of tool calls at least every three months, with the tested risk taxonomy, methodology, findings, remediation actions, and remediation timelines documented in the report.
column11: AIUC-1 control D004: third-party testing of tool calls

column6: Separate failure plans are required for security or privacy breaches, harmful outputs, and hallucinations causing customer financial loss, with owners, notification and remediation paths, preserved logs for legal review, and potential insurer activation. E004 requires accountable approval of material system changes; E008 and E009 cover internal review trails and third-party access monitoring.
column11: AIUC-1 accountability controls: AI failure plans

column6: AIUC's homepage describes a quarterly standard update focused on MCP security, third-party risk, and agent identity and permissions. The standard changes quarterly, so control citations in this tracker are re-diffed on that cadence.
column15: Event date approximated to the quarter start; AIUC labels it Q2 2026.

column6: Markets insurance for AI agents covering AI-specific losses up to $50 million, listing hallucinations, brand risk, data leakage, IP infringement, and tool-call failures such as incorrect refunds or purchase decisions. The launch announcement says cost and availability are tied directly to audit results. Policy forms, exclusions, carrier identity, and territorial details are not publicly disclosed.

ElevenLabs announced it is the first company to go live with an AIUC-1-backed policy covering AI voice agents and their actions, naming incorrect information supplied to a customer as an insurable risk, after 5,835 tests across 14 risk categories. The carrier, policy limit, premium, exclusions, and claims trigger were not disclosed.

and Attestation Tracker: Armilla with Swiss Re, Greenlight Re, and Chaucer
column6: Insurance-backed AI performance warranty based on independent evaluation of performance, fairness, robustness, and security; if a verified algorithm misses warranted KPIs, the buyer can recover the license fee under the warranty terms.

and Attestation Tracker: Armilla and Lloyd's underwriters including Chaucer
column6: Standalone third-party AI liability coverage for legal costs and liabilities arising from underperformance such as hallucinations, inaccuracies, model drift, mechanical failure, and deviations from expected behavior, described at launch as available to US insureds with global territorial limits.

Expanded limits up to $25 million per organization with express coverage descriptions for autonomous-agent failures: incorrect agent decisions, improper tool use, escalation errors, non-breach data leakage, property damage, and regulatory defense. Each policy is described as including independent AI-system certification and risk reporting.
column15: The page is dated January 21, 2026 but contains an apparent 2025 dateline typo in its body, preserved here as a source caveat.

column6: Coordinates Chaucer cyber and technology E&O with Armilla's standalone AI liability policy, using predefined allocation rules for mixed incidents, with AI liability limits described as $25 million or more per organization. Availability remains subject to underwriting and jurisdiction.

Armilla's broker page says coverage is active in North America, broker-mediated, and typically 12 months, and that quote intake requests prior AI incidents including carrier notification, dates, and remediation steps: prior-incident history is underwriting evidence.

Cyber and technology E&O for AI platforms and products, including AI product liability, generated-media liability, regulatory liability, a discrimination carveback, and deepfake crime. US products are offered through a licensed affiliate and may not be available in every state.
Excess difference-in-conditions wrap for AI-related professional negligence, IP infringement, personal injury, discrimination, privacy, bodily injury, property damage, and regulatory violations.

First-party response cover for organizations using or embedding third-party AI: business interruption, reputational harm, product recall, and response costs. The fetched materials do not disclose agent-specific trace or attestation requirements.

Performance-guarantee insurance for AI providers addressing errors and generative-AI hallucinations, able to support warranties indemnifying customers for financial losses or legal liabilities directly related to AI errors. An AI solution must undergo Munich Re's technical due diligence to obtain coverage; the public summary does not disclose a complete agent-trace data dictionary.
column15: Event date approximated to the year Munich Re says the product was pioneered.

Insurance: HSB AI liability insurance for small businesses
column6: Coverage for small and medium businesses for lawsuits involving bodily injury, property damage, and personal or advertising injury arising from AI use. As announced it was pending regulatory approval, to be added to partner carriers' business policies rather than sold directly by HSB.
column11: HSB AI liability insurance press release

Insurance: Lloyd's recognition of Armilla as an AI-liability MGA
column6: Lloyd's describes Armilla as an operating MGA and Lloyd's coverholder dedicated to AI liability, with products addressing algorithmic errors, model drift, and hallucinations: third-party corroboration of the market's existence from the marketplace itself.

Caliber Labs, PBC does business as the Artificial Intelligence Underwriting Company. Its launch announcement identified Rune Kvist, Brandon Wang, and Rajiv Dattani as founders with a $15 million seed led by Nat Friedman at NFDG. The current team page lists Kvist and Dattani; the omission of Wang is preserved as a source-history note.
Cross-sector generative-AI profile of the AI Risk Management Framework, published July 26, 2024. NIST does not characterize it as an agent-specific certification or logging specification.

An initiative for industry-led technical standards, voluntary guidance, protocol interoperability, security, and standards-gap analysis for AI agents. The fetched page describes an initiative, not a completed certifiable agent standard.
column15: Event date approximated to the year; NIST's page does not carry a precise date.

column6: Work-in-progress specification for instrumentable, traceable, inspectable agents with audit trails, a dynamic bill of materials, and inline controls, building on OpenTelemetry, OCSF, MCP, A2A, CycloneDX, SPDX, and SWID. Its OCSF mapping extends the API Activity class for agent-specific security events.

Armilla announced a live AI litigation database integrated with model evaluation and underwriting. The database endpoint, row schema, update cadence, and public-download terms were not established from the announcement; it is tracked here as the closest adjacent maintained resource.

The tribunal found Air Canada liable in negligent misrepresentation for inaccurate bereavement-fare information given by its website chatbot, holding that the airline is responsible for all the information on its website whether it comes from a static page or a chatbot, and that it did not take reasonable care to ensure its chatbot was accurate. Air Canada was ordered to pay $812.02 CAD in damages, interest, and fees. Neutral citation 2024 BCCRT 149; Tribunal Member Christopher C. Rivers.
column11: Moffatt v. Air Canada, 2024 BCCRT 149 (Civil Resolution Tribunal decisions database)
column15: A small-claims tribunal decision from British Columbia; persuasive but not binding precedent elsewhere.

Insurance: EU AI Liability Directive proposal withdrawn
column6: The European Parliament's legislative observatory records procedure 2022/0303(COD), the proposed AI Liability Directive, with status 'Procedure lapsed or withdrawn' and the event 'Proposal withdrawn by Commission' dated October 6, 2025. No successor instrument appears in the procedure file as of the tracker date.
